DFM DESIGN REVIEW

Key DFM Design Review Points for CNC Parts: Reduce Scrap and Machining Costs at the Source

A DFM review is performed before drawings are finalized and production begins, verifying that part structure, dimensions, tolerances, and wall thickness align with machining process characteristics, to reduce scrap, shorten lead times, and optimize cost.

Design Guidelines DFM CNC Parts Cost Optimization
DFM DESIGN REVIEW

The Value of DFM Review: Catch "Design Works but Is Hard to Machine" Issues Before Production

A DFM (Design for Manufacturing) review checks part structure, dimensions, tolerances, wall thickness, fillets, and fixturing approach against machining process characteristics before drawings are finalized and production begins. Effective DFM optimization can reduce machining cost, shorten cycle time, and lower scrap rates.

This article is for Structural engineers, product developers, procurement staff, CNC process engineers, and project managers
00 DFM Overview 01 Inner Corners and Fillets 02 Wall Thickness and Deep Slots 03 Tolerance Callouts 04 Fixturing Feasibility 05 Review Deliverables
00
DFM OVERVIEW

Most CNC Machining Problems Trace Back to Drawings That Ignore Machining Constraints

Many parts appear functionally sound, yet once machining begins they exhibit deformation, over-cutting, tool interference, fixturing difficulty, or excessive cost. This is not necessarily a capability gap—it often means the design did not account for CNC machining limits.

The core of DFM review is not to change part function, but to make structures easier to machine, inspect, and produce consistently at volume—without compromising performance. Typical review areas include inner corners and fillets, wall thickness and deep slots, tolerance callouts, and fixturing feasibility.

Review Area Common Issues Possible Consequences Optimization Direction
Inner Corners and Fillets Tool radius Internal cavities designed with sharp 90° corners Wire EDM or EDM required, driving up cost Add fillets matched to available tool radii
Wall Thickness and Deep Slots Thin wall / tool rigidity Walls too thin or depth-to-width ratio too high Chatter, deformation, dimensional instability Optimize wall thickness and slot depth ratio
Tolerance Callouts Functional dimensions Tight tolerances applied to non-functional areas Higher machining and inspection cost Reserve tight tolerances for critical mating surfaces
Fixturing Feasibility Datum surfaces No reliable clamping surfaces or excessive undercuts Difficult fixturing, distortion, special processes required Reserve clamping surfaces or add process tabs
01
INNER RADIUS

Inner Corner and Fillet Review: Standard Tools Cannot Produce Perfect Sharp Internal Corners

In CNC milling, end mills have a corner radius, so internal cavities cannot be cut to a perfect sharp corner. If drawings require sharp internal corners, wire EDM, sinker EDM, or other secondary processes are usually needed, significantly increasing cost and lead time.

For typical structural parts, internal fillets should match commonly available tool radii. A general guideline is internal fillet R ≥ 0.5 mm. For deep cavities, fillets can be enlarged to improve tool access, chip evacuation, and stability.

Design Recommendation

If sharp corners are not functionally required, avoid internal 90° corners. Enlarging internal fillets where structure allows reduces machining difficulty and improves surface quality.

02
WALL & SLOT

Wall Thickness and Deep Slot Review: Thin Walls and Deep Cavities Are Prone to Distortion and Vibration

Excessively thin walls can cause chatter, deformation, and dimensional drift during clamping and cutting. For aluminum, minimum wall thickness is typically recommended at ≥ 0.8 mm; stainless steel and titanium alloys offer higher rigidity and may allow thinner sections depending on part size and load.

Deep slots and cavities also depend on tool rigidity. Deeper and narrower slots increase tool overhang, leading to deflection and chatter. Keep depth-to-width ratio within roughly 4:1 where possible; beyond that, insufficient rigidity often causes machining deviation.

Design Recommendation

Add local reinforcement or optimize machining sequence for thin-wall parts. For deep slots, increase slot width, enlarge fillets, or reduce depth to limit tool overhang.

03
TOLERANCE REVIEW

Tolerance Review: Tight Tolerances Belong Only Where Function Requires Them

Tighter tolerances do not automatically mean better design. For CNC machining, high-precision tolerances increase cycle time, inspection time, and rework risk. Applying tight tolerances to every dimension on a drawing dramatically raises quoting difficulty and delivery risk.

Reserve high-precision tolerances for assembly mating surfaces—for example, critical dimensions at ≤ ±0.02 mm; non-functional cosmetic and reference dimensions can use standard tolerances. This preserves function while avoiding unnecessary machining and inspection cost.

Design Recommendation

Distinguish functional from non-functional dimensions before assigning tolerances. Assembly holes, locating surfaces, and sealing faces may warrant tight control; other areas should use reasonable general tolerances.

04
FIXTURE FEASIBILITY

Fixturing Feasibility Review: Design for How the Part Will Be Held and Machined

CNC machining requires stable workholding first. If the design lacks reliable clamping surfaces, or clamping zones are thin walls, cosmetic faces, or critical functional surfaces, distortion, clamp marks, or datum instability often appear during machining.

For small thin-wall parts, process tabs can be added for fixturing and removed after machining. Complex structures should minimize excessive undercut features. Without a viable process for undercuts, special tooling, five-axis machining, or EDM may be required.

Design Recommendation

Reserving clamping surfaces and machining datums at the drawing stage significantly reduces prototype risk. For high cosmetic requirements, avoid using visible surfaces as primary clamp zones.

05
REVIEW OUTPUT

DFM Review Deliverables Should Include Issues, Impact, and Optimization Recommendations

An effective DFM review should go beyond "machinable" or "not machinable." It should identify specific drawing issues, explain impact on cost, lead time, precision, or yield, and propose optimizations that preserve part function.

ProMakeHub offers complimentary DFM design review during the quoting stage, marking non-ideal design features and recommending improvements to optimize the machining plan without affecting part function.

DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING

Effective DFM Review Exposes Machining Risks Before Production Starts

CNC part cost, lead time, and yield depend heavily on whether drawings are manufacturable. Inner corners, wall thickness, tolerances, and fixturing details all affect actual machining outcomes. Early DFM optimization reduces rework, scrap, and unnecessary manufacturing cost.

Upload part drawings to request a complimentary DFM review and address design-driven machining risks before production.
